## AAQ: How to Achieve Success Through Spiritual Practice? | HG Suvyakta Narasimha Dasa
**Speaker:** HG Suvyakta Narasimha Dasa

### spiritual goals, devotional service, chanting
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/jlHTKoXRDmo?t=0
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "So, devotional life, Bhakti Yoga is such that it is the means as well as the end."
**Summary:** The speaker explains that in spiritual life, the long-term and short-term goal is the same: service to Krishna and Vaishnavas, achieved through chanting Hare Krishna and serving Prabhupada's mission, emphasizing that devotional service is both the means and the end.

How can I achieve my long-term and short-term goal through spiritual practice? This is the next question. You see, now it all depends on what is your goal. Is your goal material or is it spiritual? If it is spiritual, how to achieve your long-term and short-term goals? Long-term and short-term goal in spiritual life is only one: service to Krishna and service to Vaishnavas. So, we chant Hare Krishna Maha Mantra. We serve the mission of Prabhupada. That's the short-term as well as long-term goal. Life after life, we want to be engaged in the service of the mission of Srila Prabhupada. Life after life, we want Srila Prabhupada as our Diksha Guru and want to follow his instructions. And we want to chant Hare Krishna. We want to never forget Krishna's holy name. This is the short-term goal. This is the long-term goal. So, devotional life, Bhakti Yoga is such that it is the means as well as the end. The means to perfect yourself spiritually, to achieve spiritual perfection in Bhakti Yoga is to chant Hare Krishna. And after perfection, what is it that you are going to do? What is it that you are going to achieve? You will chant Hare Krishna. That's all. So, devotional service is both the means as well as the end.

---
### material goals, duty, Vedic principles
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/jlHTKoXRDmo?t=104
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "Better than working towards the result is to work for duty."
**Summary:** The speaker contrasts spiritual and material goals, arguing that focusing solely on material achievements is a regressive approach according to Vedic scriptures, and that prioritizing duty over results is a more progressive way of life.

Now, if you are talking about material goals, short-term, long-term goals, how to achieve through spiritual practice? Well, you see, this focus on achieving a certain goal, first have a goal in life, work towards it, achieve it. This is a very recent and very, what you say, regressive kind of thinking in the human society. It's not a progressive thinking. Why regressive? Because when we speak of goal, that means we have to have a result in mind. What is it that I want to achieve? So, all your activities become result-oriented. All your decisions in life will be result-oriented. Today's world people say, yes, we have to be result-oriented. Yes, that is the way to me. We have to focus. We have to have a goal. We have to work towards it, hard work, etc. But according to the Vedic scriptures, it is not a progressive way of life. Because if we focus too much on the goal that we want to achieve, we will be slipping from the human form of life into animal form of life and lower species of life. Because human life is not meant to work towards results. Better than working towards the result is to work for duty. That we also know in the material world. I mean, even in today's society, little bit of still, there is some respect for duty. Somebody wants to achieve a certain goal.

---
### duty, Dronacharya, Vedic education
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/jlHTKoXRDmo?t=257
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "Dronacharya thought, what is my duty in this circumstance? I am a teacher. He is a qualified student seeking knowledge. I cannot deny him. I will teach him."
**Summary:** The speaker illustrates the importance of duty over results by narrating the story of Dronacharya, who taught archery to Drishtadyumna, even though he knew Drishtadyumna was destined to kill him, highlighting the Vedic principle of imparting knowledge to qualified students regardless of personal consequences.

Focus is on doing one's duty. The best example of this is Dronacharya. Dronacharya was a teacher. And his duty was to teach any eligible, qualified candidate if he comes. That's the duty of the teacher to teach. And in the Vedic times, teachers would not work for money. They would not say, give me a certain piece, then I will teach you. Today's world, teaching means only qualification required is you have to have money to pay the teacher. You have money to pay the teacher, college, whatever, you will get educated. That's the only thing. No other consideration is there. But in the Vedic times, money was never in the picture. The teacher will never ask for money. Teacher will assess, is the student qualified to receive this knowledge and will he use this knowledge properly. If yes, then the teacher will teach him even if he doesn't get any fees in return. And after receiving the knowledge, the student will not just walk away. Today's world, what others? Go to college, get educated, convocation will be there, go, take a degree, walk out after that, you are not bothered. You will not even remember the name of your teacher. But in the Vedic times, it is not like that. If you have received knowledge, then the student at the end of it, although teacher is not asking for any fees, it is the duty of the disciple to give some Guru Dakshina. Dakshina means it is whatever it is possible for you, you give. It is not a fee. It is a Dakshina. From my side, I am giving according to what level of gratitude I feel and what is possible for me, what is my capacity, I am giving. So it is Guru Dakshina. So the student has to give. Guru Dakshina, he cannot come away empty handed. So the teacher also, whatever possible for you, give. I give like that. So Dronacharya was approached by Drishtadyumna. Who is this Drishtadyumna? Drishtadyumna was the son of Drupada. And Drupada had some fight with Dronacharya. And Drupada had conducted a yajna to beget a son who would kill Drona. And that is how Drishtadyumna was born. So Drishtadyumna was born to kill Dronacharya. And this Drishtadyumna comes to Dronacharya and says, I want to learn the art of archery from you. I want to learn Dhanur Vidya from you. And what does Dronacharya do? Dronacharya says, yes, I will teach him. Dronacharya knows very well that Drishtadyumna is born to kill him. So if Dronacharya were to be result oriented, what he will think? If I teach him, what is the result? I will kill him. So I will not teach him. Correct knowledge. That is the common sense. Anybody in today's world, he will think like that. And you cannot fault him. But Dronacharya did not think like that. Dronacharya thought, what is my duty in this circumstance? I am a teacher. He is a qualified student seeking knowledge. I cannot deny him. I will teach him. So impartially he taught Drishtadyumna also along with the Pandavas and Kauravas.

---
### duty, Duryodhana, Pandavas, Dharma
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/jlHTKoXRDmo?t=452
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "So in Vedic civilization, the whole fight of the Kurukshetra happened because Duryodhana was result oriented, Pandavas were duty oriented."
**Summary:** The speaker contrasts Duryodhana's result-oriented approach with the Pandavas' duty-oriented approach, explaining that the Kurukshetra war was a consequence of Duryodhana's desire for the kingdom, while the Pandavas prioritized their duty as Kshatriyas, even when it meant facing hardship.

So duty. In Vedic civilization, duty was the foremost. If you look at Duryodhana, Duryodhana was result oriented. I want the kingdom. Somehow I have to get the kingdom. Either cheat, fight, do whatever, get the kingdom. Result oriented. Pandavas, duty oriented. We will do our duty. Whatever our duty requires of us, we will. Result is immaterial. So Duryodhana cheated and got the kingdom. Pandavas also could have cheated and kept the kingdom. But they said, no, we will not cheat. We will do our duty as a Kshatriya. We have lost the game of dice. We have won in Vannavasa and Ajnatavasa. We will do it. So they were like that. So in Vedic civilization, the whole fight of the Kurukshetra happened. Why? Because Duryodhana was result oriented. Pandavas were duty oriented. That is why it was a Dharma Yudha. Because Duryodhana was not following Dharma. Pandavas were following Dharma. So the higher than being a result oriented worker is to be a duty oriented worker.

---
### duty, Varnashrama Dharma, Sanatana Dharma, sacrifice
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/jlHTKoXRDmo?t=535
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "So all these duties, if somebody follows, that is higher than simply doing duty according to one's livelihood."
**Summary:** The speaker elaborates on the concept of duty, stating that following duty according to Varnashrama Dharma and Sanatana Dharma is superior to simply fulfilling one's professional obligations, and that duties extend to other living entities, Devatas, Rishis, and include Yajna, Dana, and Tapasya.

And in duty oriented work also, following the duty according to the Varnashama Dharma, Sanatana Dharma given in the Vedic scriptures, that is even better than executing duty according to our understanding in today's world. I have to go to office on time. I have to work 8 hours. I have to do my whatever assigned tasks are there. That is one level of duty. But according to Varnashama Dharma, I have to situate myself in a particular Varna and Ashrama and perform those duties. Those duties are not limited only to the office work. The duties extend not only to one's office or one's vocation, one's livelihood. But also to work. But also to the other living entities, to other human beings, to the Devatas, to the Rishis and Sages, to even the animals in the society like cows and dogs and bulls and horses which from all these animals will take so much service. So there are duties to perform towards them also. And over and above all this, Yajna, Dana and Tapasya. There are duties towards sacrifice for the Devatas and for Vishnu. Then there is Dana, charity. One has to compulsorily give charity. That is one of the duties. If you are earning money, it is not that you have to put it in your bank and you have to enjoy. No. You have to also give Dana. That is also a charity. Charity is also a duty. And Tapasya. Not only that. You also have to undertake certain austerities. Tapasyas. You have to fast on Ekadashi. You have to fast on Janmashtami. You have to follow certain Pratas. So certain Tapasyas are also built in. So all these duties, if somebody follows, that is higher than simply doing duty according to one's livelihood.

---
### surrender, exclusive devotion, Krishna
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/jlHTKoXRDmo?t=662
**Verse References:** BG 18.66
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees
**Key Quote:** "Your only duty should be to serve Krishna."
**Summary:** The speaker explains that the highest form of duty is to give up all other duties and exclusively serve Krishna, referencing Krishna's instruction in the Bhagavad Gita to surrender unto Him alone, emphasizing that the ultimate goal is to serve Krishna and the spiritual master without concern for personal liberation or destination.

Now out of all the people who perform duties, according to the Varnashrama Dharma, those who give up all those duties, and make it their only duties to serve Krishna exclusively, he is even higher. That is why Krishna tells in the end of the Bhagavad Gita, here in chapter 66 verse, sarva dharman parityajya. Give up all other duties. sarva dharman parityajya mam ekam saranam vraja. Surrender unto only me. Your only duty should be to serve Krishna. So the person who makes it the goal of his life and the existence of his life, his duty of his life is only exclusively serving Krishna. Serving Krishna means serving the duty of the spiritual master, Srila Prabhupada. So a person who does this, he is the topmost. Even there also there is no goal. There is no goal that we have to achieve. I have to go back to the garden. I have to achieve liberation. I should go to Vaikuntha. None of those things should be there. My only goal is my duty which is to serve Krishna and my Guru Maharaj, Srila Prabhupada. That's all. Where I go, how many births I will take, whether I get liberated or not, none of my consideration.

---
### human faculty, duty, self-restraint, austerity
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/jlHTKoXRDmo?t=751
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "So, duty means you should be ready even if something, I have to do something, I have to do something which I don't like to do, I have to still do. That is called duty."
**Summary:** The speaker emphasizes the unique human capacity for duty, self-restraint, and sacrifice, explaining that duty involves doing what is not pleasing, and that developing this faculty leads to higher levels of spiritual development, culminating in exclusive service to Krishna.

So that is the topmost. Development of the human intellect and human faculty. You can go up till there. Because only human beings have this faculty, you see. To think of duty. Animals have no sense of duty. That is why you don't have dharma for them. That you have to fast on Ekadashi. Can you go and tell your pet dog or pet cat, today is Ekadashi fast. You can forcibly make the cat or dog eat Ekadashi Prasastu. That's a difficult matter. But you know, it's not that the cat or dog is voluntarily following. No, we are voluntarily following. So that faculty to take tapasya, that faculty to sacrifice, that faculty to undertake certain austerities, the faculty to exercise self-restraint, is there only in the human form. So if you use that capability, that faculty in the human being, to do things which are not one's liking. I may like to do something, but I say, no, I will not do that. I will do something which is not very pleasing. If you ask me what I like to do, I like to eat when I feel hungry. But today is Janmasthami, I have to fast. So, although you would like to eat, you are ready to sacrifice that desire to eat and you are ready to fast. That faculty is there in the human being to do something, to give up something which you like to do and to do something which is not very pleasing. This faculty is there in the human being. And that is where that sense of duty springs from. In duty, what is there? Duty, you will do your duty even if it is not palatable, even if it is distressing. So, duty means you should be ready even if something, I have to do something, I have to do something which I don't like to do, I have to still do. That is called duty. So, you develop this faculty in the human being of self-restraint. You develop from the point of you know, doing your duty to doing varnashama dharma duty to rendering exclusive service to Krishna. That is the topmost development of the faculty.

---
### devotional service, chanting, Prabhupada, grace
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/jlHTKoXRDmo?t=895
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** practical
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "That chanting of Krishna's holy name and rendering service will gradually pull you towards that final destination of your devotional service."
**Summary:** The speaker concludes by stating that the real goal is to reach a stage where one's only duty is to serve Krishna and Guru, achieved through chanting Hare Krishna, associating with devotees, and rendering practical devotional service, emphasizing that this process is driven by the taste for Krishna and the grace of Krishna and Guru.

So, achieving long term, short term, goals is at the very bottom level. We have to progress far beyond that. We have to go to a certain level of sense of duty and in that also we have to go to duty towards varnashama dharma and finally duty towards guru and Krishna. That is the topmost decision. So, we should instead of thinking of short term, long term material goals, we should be thinking of when will I come to the stage where my only duty will be to serve Krishna and Guru. No other duty in my life. That is the real goal that we have to have. And how to achieve that? Very simple. Chant Hare Krishna, associate with devotees and render whatever practical devotional service you can. That chanting of Krishna's holy name and rendering service will gradually pull you towards that final destination of your devotional service. Where you have no other motivation than to serve the pleasure of Guru and Krishna. So, rendering devotional service to Krishna is very powerful. We don't have to force ourselves. Oh no, I have to chant sixteen rounds. You don't have to do that. It's not a matter of force. We all begin, most of us, we begin by chanting one round, two rounds. Slowly we will make it four rounds. Slowly make it eight rounds. Then we will come to sixteen rounds. Isn't it? So, how does that happen? It's not that somebody is standing behind us with a stick. If you don't chant four rounds today, from today onwards I am going to beat you. Nobody did that. We automatically, we felt a taste for chanting. And we felt yes, I want to chant four rounds from today. Start chanting. So, the taste for Krishna is like that. Once you taste the devotional service, that devotional service will drag you until you come to the final point of your devotional service. So, simple thing. Read Prabhupada's books. Follow Prabhupada's instructions. Chant Hare Krishna. And you will achieve your long-term goal, which is to become a pure devotee of Krishna. That's the only result you can, you can desire. That also, it is dependent on the grace of Krishna and Buddha. Whether they give award as pure devotional service or not. But as far as we are concerned, we want to serve our Guru's instructions and Krishna's instructions. That's a period. Whether we get Krishna Prema or not, whether I go to the spiritual world or not, whether I get liberated or not, these things are not our consideration.

---

## AAQ: How to Serve Krishna Without Feeling Overwhelmed | His Grace Suvyakta Narasimha Dasa
**Speaker:** HG Suvyakta Narasimha Dasa

### false ego, humility, service
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/HwUSa66RoYw?t=101
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "But if that devotee had not done it that day, today I will not be sitting here."
**Summary:** The speaker shares a personal anecdote about overcoming his false ego through the encouragement of another devotee to engage in menial service, highlighting the transformative impact of such experiences.

I am for program. I went, kept that in the corner, slowly started walking towards the door. Again, Prabhuji, Prabhuji! I said, now what? I said, where are you? He said, where are you going? There are so many more mats. I said, what, one mat is not enough? You want me to pick up more mats? Appa, it was such a, I tell you, it was a, you know, for my ego, because all my life I have never done any menial work like that. Lived always like a prince, like a king, you know, there are always people to take care of washing the clothes and washing the dishes and all that. Never did any such thing in my life. So it was really, you know, it was really, it was like somebody is pushing me, very uncomfortable doing that and all that. But if that devotee had not done it that day, today I will not be sitting here. That's the reality. So I'm thankful to him that he broke through my false ego barrier and he pushed me to engage in service because of which today I'm here.

---
### devotional service, false ego, gratitude
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/HwUSa66RoYw?t=224
**Verse References:** BG 9
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "The pain that you have to take in Krishna consciousness is the pain of overcoming our false ego, which always advises us against rendering service to Krishna."
**Summary:** The speaker explains that false ego prevents us from rendering service to Krishna, causing discomfort and reluctance, and emphasizes the importance of gratitude towards those who push us beyond our comfort zones into devotional service.

So reality is, what is preventing us from rendering service to Krishna? Why are we feeling reluctant? Why are we feeling burdensome? Krishna actually says in the ninth chapter of the Bhagavad Gita, susukam kartum abhyayam. Devotional service to Krishna is very joyfully performed. So then if it is joyfully performed, when picking up the mat, why am I feeling so much discomfort, so much embarrassment? Because false ego. Maya doesn't want us to engage in service to Krishna, because if we engage in service, we'll get out of our clutches. So Maya will use false ego and tell us, why you are to do all these things? This is not meant for you. You are supposed to live like a king. What is all this, you know, picking up the mat, wiping the floor? No, no, no, no, no, no, no. These are all burdens. And you are engaging in spiritual life for this. I mean, you are an engineer, you have studied in so much, this one big college, you have got so much rank, this, that, and all that. All that for this? So these are the thoughts that will come to your mind and which will inhibit you, which will discourage you from doing service to Krishna. So at that time, some devotee has to come and push you. Baba, do know. So little by little, little by little, that barrier gets broken, broken, broken, broken. What will happen is eventually we'll become comfortable. We'll go beyond comfortable. We'll start loving to do that service. So this is how we have to be ready to go beyond our comfort zone. Then only we can expect to progress. No pain, no gain. The pain that you have to take in Krishna consciousness is the pain of overcoming our false ego, which always advises us against rendering service to Krishna. So therefore, if somebody is pushing you into services, you should not develop grudge against that devotee. You should be grateful to that devotee. You see my false ego, Maya is so strongly preventing me from doing service, but this devotee so mercifully, is pushing me to engage in service to Krishna. That is the way we should look at it. So it is good that somebody is pushing you out of your comfort zone.

---
### comfort zone, spiritual progress, humility
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/HwUSa66RoYw?t=373

**Content Type:** practical
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers

If you want to achieve something in life, you have to push yourself beyond the comfort zone. Then only you will achieve something. should be ready to slog not we are not here coming to spiritual life or comfortable life please take it out of your system that I am coming here for comfortable life to feel good no when you come to Krishna consciousness you'll get battered your false ego will get smashed you will you know you will you will you will have to do the most menial services you'll have to pick up footwear you'll have to wipe the somebody else's juta the they have eaten and left you have to wipe that floor all these things you will have to do in fact you know I should not share this it may put up many of the devotees who have ideas of joining the temple but still I will tell you so that tomorrow you have no disillusionment the first service when anybody joins the temple whether he is from IIT or from MIT first service he gets when he joins the ashram is cleaning the toilets in the ashram even I also when I joined one first as soon as I joined first one full week was cleaning all the toilets and bathrooms in the ashram and Bangalore ashram is very big there are you know 50 60 devotees at least 10 bathrooms and 10 sets of bathrooms and toilets each set will have 10 bathrooms and 10 toilets like that 10 sets of common bathrooms or toilets are there which whole week I had to clean so this is our beginning in spiritual life we have to learn to be humble we have to learn to do services which are not in our comfort zone our idea of into spiritual life is not to please ourselves our idea of coming to Krishna consciousness is to please Krishna and guru so if you have to please Krishna and guru we should not look at ourselves what is comfortable for me we are go beyond that and so that training begins with somebody pushing you and telling you to go beyond your comfort zone and engaging in service so if you are in Krishna consciousness get ready to be pushed around simple.

And if you are not in Krishna consciousness get ready to be pushed around that is the way to become sincere in devotional service we are all not born with sincerity in devotional service you please understand we are all not born with sincerity we are all being saved by Prahupad it's not that we are all sincerely coming and serving Krishna we want to please Krishna that's not our mood to come to that place here to become a pure devotee so Prahupad is very compassionately picking us up and saving us we have to when we when we are forcibly engaging ourselves in service to Krishna and guru Shaila Prahupad eventually sincerity will come as our heart gets cleansed as we become purified more and more of the contamination that I am this body as as we get purified of this contamination we will get more and more comfortable in doing our services which shows our sincerity all right.

## AAQ: How to end the Cycle of Birth & Death? | His Grace Suvyakta Narasimha Dasa
**Speaker:** HG Suvyakta Narasimha Dasa

### cycle of birth and death, subtle body, reincarnation
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/fmJV0kQW6bU?t=0
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "So same logic, if you extrapolate, at the time of death, this body will cease to be, I will go on to another body."
**Summary:** The speaker explains the cycle of birth and death, using the analogy of changing bodies within a single lifetime and the role of the subtle body in carrying the soul to the next life.

After death, an interesting question. After death, the human soul has to take another body. When will this process stop, and what do we do for that? So Krishna tells in the Bhagavad Gita that within this very life, you can see that we are changing so many bodies. I gave up the body of a baby, got the body of a child, gave up the body of the child, got the body of a youth, gave up the body of the youth, got an old body, got a new body, got an old person's body, and finally when death comes, I will give up this body. So logically speaking, when in this life I have changed so many bodies, but I continue to exist. I didn't cease to exist. The body of the baby finished means I was not finished, I continued, right? So same logic, if you extrapolate, at the time of death, this body will cease to be, I will go on to another body. So when death comes, what happens? We are taken by our subtle body. Mind, intelligence, and false ego transfer us to another body. Actually, that's what has happened to us in this life also. When the body of the baby went away and we got the body of the child, we continue to feel I am the same person. And when we get a youthful body, we continue to feel I am the same person, although the body of the child and the baby is not there. Why? Because the same subtle body is continuing. The same mind, intelligence, and false ego is continuing. That is continuing uninterrupted. So for me, that feeling of I, I am this, doesn't cease. So we don't feel that I am changing the body, because although the gross body is changing, the subtle body is not changing. So the subtle body carries us from the body of the baby to the body of the child to the body of the youth to the body of the old person. Similarly, the subtle body will carry us from this body to the next body. So this cycle of birth and death goes on, correct?

---
### suffering, birth, death, disease, old age
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/fmJV0kQW6bU?t=147
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "So whether it is birth or death, it is very painful."
**Summary:** The speaker emphasizes the painful nature of birth, death, disease, and old age, highlighting the suffering inherent in the material world.

So Krishna tells in the Bhagavad Gita, jātasya dhruva mṛtyur. Whenever a person is born, 100% guaranteed that that person will die. Yes or no? Everybody accepts this. And Krishna says the reverse is also true. Jātasya dhruva mṛtyur janma mṛtasya ca. When a person dies, it is guaranteed that he is going to take another birth. So we are all caught up in this cycle of repeated birth and death. It's not a pleasurable thing to undergo birth and death. Death is a very, very painful experience. That's why nobody wants to die. Even an animal understands this. So if somebody, if a human being says, what is there? Death, you know, it's not a big thing. I am not afraid of death. That means he is duller than an animal. Even an animal understands death is very painful. That's why you try to cut the throat of an animal, it will shout, it will shriek, it will scream, it will try to run away. It will not give up so easily because even an animal knows death is very painful. So death is a very painful thing. Similarly, birth is also very painful. How are all of us born? We are all born crying, no? None of us are born, ahahaha, you know, laughing, you know, I had a wonderful experience, so joyful to take birth. Nobody takes birth like that, right? Everybody takes birth crying. It's an extremely painful experience. The pain is so great. It's so painful, the process of birth. So whether it is birth or death, it is very painful. And if you say, okay, momentarily, I have to undergo some pain for nine months, 10 months, I'm in the mother's womb, painful, but after I come out, over, no pain. And death, few seconds, it will be over. Okay. So, you know, birth, when I took birth, some pain I underwent, some suffering I underwent, 100 years I lived, happy. And at the end of my life, I was happy. And at the end of 100 years, death came, I had to struggle a little bit, no problem. No, sir, in between birth and death, two more things are there, old age and disease. That is also painful, no? One small fever comes also, it will be so depressing for us. We have to take medication, the body will ache, there will be a headache, eyes will be burning, the nose will be burning. So many problems. We will not be able to digest proper food. Taste will go away. So it's painful. Disease is painful. And old age is also painful. All the joints will ache, there will be no energy in the body, you will not be able to walk, you cannot sit down, you cannot walk properly. So much trouble.

---
### suffering, threefold miseries, kleshas
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/fmJV0kQW6bU?t=312
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "So you see, in the midst of this birth and death, so many miseries we have to undergo."
**Summary:** The speaker elaborates on the threefold miseries (kleshas) - adhibhautika, adhidaivika, and adhyatmika - explaining how they contribute to suffering in the material world, using examples like natural disasters, political issues, and mental distress.

And as if these four things are not enough, there are also three kinds of kleshas, adhibhautika klesha, adhidaivika klesha, and adhyatmika klesha. Adhidaivika klesha, excessive redness, headaches, empty brains, floods. You built a house with great difficulty. You earned a lot of money, you did hard work, built a house, one flood will come, everything is swept away. Gone. Nobody has control over this. Supernatural occurrences. Distress, miseries which come on account of supernatural forces. Floods, earthquakes, extreme heat during summer, extreme cold during winter, all these things will create problems for us. Okay, all the rainy season got over, the weather has become very nice and you know October, November season very, very pleasant weather. So things have become peaceful. No Adi Bhavathika Klesa. Immediately what will happen? There will be Adi Daivika Klesa is not there. Adi Bhavathika Klesa will start. Trump will announce H1B visa, $100,000 you have to pay and he will say 50% tariff I am putting exactly when Diwali is starting. So all your, you know, festival mood is all dampened. Arrey, I did not do anything to Trump. Why is he putting tariff on me? So other living entities will cause problems for you. And you will see when it is extreme heat during summer or extreme cold during winter, what will happen? You will be troubled by winter. The heat and the cold of the winter but there will be no mosquitoes. Weather becomes very good, immediately mosquitoes will start. One problem goes another will come. Adi Bhavathika Klesa. Other living entities will come and trouble us for no reason, for no fault of ours. And then there is Adhyatmika Klesa. Everything is fine, but my own mind will give me trouble. I will become depressed for no reason. Adi Bhavathika Klesa. Adi Bhavathika Klesa. I will become depressed for no reason. Adi Bhavathika Klesa. Adi Bhavathika no reason. Mood is not good today. I feel very grumpy today. You cannot help it, isn't it? Everybody around you is happy. You alone are feeling very, you know, very foul mood today. So everybody will start avoiding it. Today's mood is not okay. Don't go near it.

---
### liberation, Krishna consciousness, devotional service
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/fmJV0kQW6bU?t=461
**Verse References:** BG 4.9
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "So an intelligent person will question, how can I get out of this cycle of birth and death and disease, old age and these threefold miseries? What should I do?"
**Summary:** The speaker presents Krishna consciousness as the solution to escape the cycle of birth and death and the threefold miseries, emphasizing the importance of understanding Krishna's transcendental nature and engaging in devotional service.

So our own mind is giving trouble. Adhyatmika plays. So you see, in the midst of this birth and death, so many miseries we have to undergo. So an intelligent person will question, how can I get out of this cycle of birth and death and disease, old age and these threefold miseries? What should I do? So if you want to do that, Krishna says in the Bhagavad Gita, janma karma chamedivyam evam yojanati tatvatah, tyaktvadeham punarjanma naiti mameti sorjuna. Krishna says that if a soul takes the trouble to understand the position of Krishna, that Krishna, his birth, his activities, his qualities, these are all not material. Krishna's birth is not like my birth. Krishna doesn't have to undergo old age and disease like me. Krishna doesn't have to undergo Adhyatmika klesha, Adibhavitika klesha, Adidaivika klesha. Krishna is beyond all this. Simply to understand that Krishna is transcendent, his activities are transcendental, his birth is transcendental, his every quality is transcendental, simply by understanding this, Krishna says, punarjanma naiti, you don't have to take repeated birth and death in this material world. Then what will happen to us? Mameti, Krishna says, we will go to Krishna, we will go to Krishna loka. Just like we all live on this earth planet, Krishna has his own home, which is called Goloka Vrindavan. We will go there. Now, if we go there, what is the advantage? Whether I go there or here, both places I am existing. So, what happens to the troubles of birth, death, disease, old age and the Adidaivika klesha, Adibhavitika klesha, Adhyatmika klesha, what about all these things? Krishna says that once you go to the spiritual world, that Goloka Vrindavan Vaikuntha, there is no more birth, death, disease, old age. And the three-fold miseries there don't exist in the material, in the spiritual world. So, therefore, if you want to get out of the cycle of birth and death, you don't want another birth within the material world, then what you should do? You should learn about Krishna from the Bhagavad Gita and Srimad Bhagavatam and follow the instructions of Krishna. And if you follow the instructions of Krishna, then no more the problems of birth, death, disease and old age. All right. So, the simple solution to this repeated birth and death is engaging in devotional service to Krishna. That's the simple thing.

---

## AAQ: Is Evil The Highest Ignorance? Does It Have a Form? | His Grace Suvyakta Narasimha Dasa
**Speaker:** HG Suvyakta Narasimha Dasa

### relativity, ethics, cultural norms
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/_7OBH-Y_or8?t=0
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "The standard of what is evil, what is not evil, what is good, what is bad, all these things keep changing with time."
**Summary:** The speaker discusses how the definition of evil and good changes over time, citing examples like alcohol consumption, illicit relationships, and gambling, which were once considered sinful but are now often seen as normal.

Is evil the highest degree of ignorance? Does evil have a personified form? Is evil the highest degree of ignorance? Well, what constitutes being evil? We may have different concepts of what is evil and what is good. In the olden times, in the Vedic times, to consume alcohol, to have an illicit relationship outside of marriage between man and woman, this was considered sinful. To engage in gambling was considered sinful. Today the definition has changed. Man and woman, outside of marriage, they have a relationship, consensual, it's all fine. Who are we to interfere? It is their private matter. We see online so many betting apps and betting channels and so many things are going on. Even online, openly it is all being advertised. Come and play and gamble. Intoxication, I mean, who doesn't intoxicate now? So today all these things are not considered sinful. They are all considered normal, that's part of normal life. So, they stand up. The standard of what is evil, what is not evil, what is good, what is bad, all these things keep changing with time.

---
### ethics, cultural norms, punishment
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/_7OBH-Y_or8?t=127
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "Evilness is... the definition of evilness keeps changing with time."
**Summary:** The speaker uses the example of Ravana's abduction of Sita to illustrate how perceptions of evil change over time, contrasting ancient Vedic standards with modern legal systems and societal norms.

If you look at Ravana in his time, he was considered an evil man because he abducted Sita against her will and he imprisoned her. And when Ramachandra came to claim her back, he refused to return her and fought a battle to death with him. By today's standards, if you see, I mean, Ravana just abducted her, that's all. He didn't do anything else. So by today's standards, Ravana is a very good man. He was very honorable. He kept telling Sita Devi, marry me. He did not violate her in any other way. But in his time, what he did was evil. To abduct a woman against her will, he had to give up his life. So, in Vedic times, even abduction of a woman was considered so evil that anybody who would do that could be sentenced to death. That was the culture at that time. Today, you know, it's not a major thing. Somebody, even if he forces himself upon a woman, even if he rapes her, maximum the court will give some few years of imprisonment. After that, he is free. And that imprisonment also will come after decades of trial. So many years of trial, then Sessions Court, then the High Court, then the Supreme Court. Practically, by that time, the man who committed the crime will already be on his death bed. What is the use of all this sentencing? So in today's world, maximum punishment that a man can get for violating a woman's modesty is a few years of imprisonment. That's all. It's not considered all that evil. Whereas, Ravana, just for abducting Sita, he had to give up his life. He was punished by taking his life. So, according to Shastra, Atataina, seven kinds of aggressors are there. One of them is one who abducts another's wife. He is considered an Atatain, aggressor. And all such aggressors can be given the death penalty. So, evilness is...

---
### relativity, Manodharma, cultural norms
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/_7OBH-Y_or8?t=287
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "What is good at one time becomes bad at another. What is bad at one time becomes good at another time."
**Summary:** The speaker continues to illustrate the changing nature of good and evil, using examples like intoxication and live-in relationships, and introduces the concept of Manodharma, stating that our perceptions of good and bad are mental concoctions that change with time and culture.

The definition of evilness keeps changing with time, according to society's perception of what is right, wrong, changing cultures, societal norms, etc. Similarly, if you look at intoxication. Hundred years back in India, anybody intoxicates, the whole society would look at him with abhorrence. Oh, this man is a drunkard. But today, it is a prestige matter to drink. You go to any rich man's house, there will be one section, a bar with all kinds of alcoholic drinks. It is prestigious to have. If you go to a rich man's house and there is no bar, then it looks very bad. What is this man? He is not even having a bar in his house. You can imagine. Today, the perception has changed. People's values have changed. That is why in Chaitanya Charitamrita, Krishnadas Kaviraj Goswami says, 'This is good, this is bad.' What we think this is good and this is bad in the material world is all Manodharma. It is all our mental concoction. According to the circumstances, we say this is good, this is bad. Some years later, our perception changes, our value system changes, our culture changes. We will say no. What we considered bad earlier is actually good. And what we considered good till now is bad. Hundred years back, in India, if you spoke of live-in relationships, very bad. Marriage, good. Today, if you speak of marriage, very bad. Live-in relationship, very good. Changes. People's perception, people's value system changes. What is good at one time becomes bad at another. What is bad at one time becomes good at another time. So, there is this. This keeps on changing. So, you cannot say what is permanently good and what is permanently bad. This is all because of our mental concoction. Prabhupada gives an example, just like wet stool and dry stool. If we say this is wet stool, this is bad. This is dry stool. Dry stool is good. Does it have any meaning? Wet stool, dry stool, all bad only. Similarly, this is good, this is bad. All this consideration in the material world, everything is born of ignorance. Though this, we are, somebody is helping other people, somebody is doing good to others, somebody is doing charity. Oh, these are all good things. Somebody is stealing, somebody is harming others, somebody is cheating others, these are all bad things. All good, bad is good. All good, bad, everything is bad only in the material world. Everything is born of ignorance only. We have created our own mano dharmas, our own dharmas, our own duties and what is right and wrong in this material world out of our own mental concoctions arising out of our bodily concept of life which is a result of our ignorance.

---
### devotion, surrender, knowledge
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/_7OBH-Y_or8?t=484
**Verse References:** BG 18.66, BG 7.19
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "The only eternally good thing is surrendering unto Krishna."
**Summary:** The speaker concludes that both good and evil in the material world are born of ignorance, and that only devotional service to Krishna, or surrendering to Krishna, is truly good and arises from enlightenment, referencing Bhagavad Gita verses to support this point.

So, it is not a matter of only evil being born of ignorance. Even whatever we think good is also born out of ignorance. Only devotional service to Krishna is outside the purview of ignorance. Therefore, Krishna tells in the Bhagavad Gita, sarva dharman parityajya mam ekam saranam vraja. Give up all other types of concocted duties. Simply surrender unto me, Krishna says. This means surrendering to Krishna is the only really good thing. Only really thing which is arising out of enlightenment. That is why Krishna also tells in the Bhagavad Gita. What does Krishna say? Krishna says that bahunam janmanam ante jnanavan, meaning a person who is in knowledge. One who has cultivated spiritual knowledge for many many lifetimes. bahunam janmanam ante jnanavan mam prapadyate vasudevaha sarvam iti samahatma sudurlabhah. If you are really not in ignorance, if you are really in jnana, knowledge, then you should be surrendering to Krishna saying that Krishna is everything. If I serve Krishna, then all other duties are fulfilled. If I don't surrender to Krishna, no matter whether I am evil or good in the eyes of the material people, it is all ignorance. Alright. So therefore, it's not a matter of evil being the highest degree of ignorance. The ignorance is our non-surrendering to Krishna. Non-submission to Krishna is ignorance. Rest, good, evil, bad, all these things, it's all changing from time to time. There is no set standard. There is no eternally good, eternally evil thing. The only eternally good thing is surrendering unto Krishna. That's all. Everything else is born of ignorance. Alright.

---

## AAQ: Is It Okay to Chant Without Mala or While Wearing Shoes? | His Grace Suvyakta Narasimha Dasa
**Speaker:** HG Suvyakta Narasimha Dasa

### holy name, mercy, self-realization
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/WNEYmKvVJqM?t=0
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers, General public
**Key Quote:** "Simply by doing this, the forest fire which is burning our heart of repeated birth and death within this material world will be vanquished and we will be able to see everything properly in full enlightenment with self-realization that I am not the body, I am spirit-soul."
**Summary:** Chanting the holy name of Krishna is a merciful gift that cleanses the mind, leading to self-realization and liberation from the cycle of birth and death.

Can I chant without mala and can I chant while walking with slippers? Chaitanya Mahaprabhu in his Sixth Ashtaka, he says, O Krishna, you are very, very merciful. Why? Because you have given your holy name. And your holy name can be chanted by everyone in the material world today. And by chanting the holy name, we can develop love of Krishna. This is your great kripa. That when the holy name rises in our heart, what happens? The mirror of our mind gets cleansed. Our perception of the material world becomes clearer and clearer. We are able to see, I am not the body, I am a spirit soul. We are able to see that material sense creativity, material sense education has no value. We are able to perceive that going back to Godhead is the goal of life. So all these perceptions come about by the cleansing of the mirror of the mind. And because of this cleansing of the mind of the heart, what is it that we are achieving? Chetodarpana marjanam bhava maha dava agni nirvapanam. The intense forest fire of repeated material birth and death gets vanquished simply by chanting Krishna's holy name. Param vijayate sri krishna sankirtanam. So Krishna is very merciful. He has given his holy name simply by the process of chanting Hare Krishna. No big endeavor required. Simply we are to chant Hare Krishna, Hare Krishna, Krishna Krishna, Hare Hare, Hare Rama Hare, Rama Rama Rama Hare Hare. Simply by doing this, the forest fire which is burning our heart of repeated birth and death within this material world will be vanquished and we will be able to see everything properly in full enlightenment with self-realization that I am not the body, I am spirit-soul.

---
### holy name, power, compassion
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/WNEYmKvVJqM?t=166
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers, General public
**Key Quote:** "And in his holy name Krishna has invested all his power."
**Summary:** Krishna has invested all His power into His holy name, making it as potent as His personal presence.

And to chant the holy names of Krishna, Nam Namakari Bahuda. And to chant the holy name of Krishna, Krishna has unlimited names. Not just one name. Whatever holy name of Krishna we can chant and we will immediately feel that the pangs of material existence are reducing, are diminishing. So Nam Namakari Bahuda, innumerable names Krishna has given so that we can chant any one of them and we can immediately gain liberation from the burning forest fire of material distress. Nam Namakari Bahuda, Nija Sarva Shakti. And in his holy name Krishna has invested all his power. So when we chant Krishna's holy name, that holy name of Krishna has as much power as if Krishna was personally standing there. This is an extremely wonderful thing. So you see the kripa of Krishna. This is the kripa of Krishna. This is the compassion of Krishna. That he has invested all his powers in his holy name. Simply by chanting Krishna's holy name, Krishna, along with all his Shakti's, potencies is present there. Nam Namakari Bahuda, Nija Sarva Shakti.

---
### chanting, rules, liberation
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/WNEYmKvVJqM?t=244
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers, General public
**Key Quote:** "There is no rule and regulation to be followed in chanting Krishna's holy name. Anywhere and everywhere, all the time, 24 bar 7. You can chant Krishna's holy name."
**Summary:** There are no strict rules for chanting Krishna's holy name; it can be done anytime, anywhere, and in any condition.

Tatrarpita Niyamitah Smarane Nakalaha. Nakalaha Nanyamaha. There is no rule and regulation to be followed in chanting Krishna's holy name. Anywhere and everywhere, all the time, 24 bar 7. You can chant Krishna's holy name. And there is no consideration of place, Kala and time. No, it has to be chanted only during the three Sandhya. I cannot chant during evening. I should not chant during night time. No such restriction exists in chanting the holy name of Krishna. So, to chant Krishna's holy name, to chant Hare Krishna, there are no hard and fast rules and regulations. You can wear your slipper and walk and chant Hare Krishna. You can remove your slipper and walk and chant Hare Krishna. You can wear your slipper and sit on a chair and chant Hare Krishna. You can remove your slipper and chant, sit and chant Hare Krishna. Whatever condition you want, you can sit, you can chant Hare Krishna. Whether you have a mala, you don't have a mala, you are walking, you are running, you are driving, you are swimming, you are bathing, you are eating, you are sleeping, whatever condition you want, you can sit, you can chant Hare Krishna. Whether you have a mala, you don't have a mala, you are walking, you are running, you are driving, you are swimming, you are bathing, you are eating, you are sleeping, whatever condition you are in, you can chant Hare Krishna.

---
### chanting, vow, progress
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/WNEYmKvVJqM?t=333
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** practical
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Although chanting Hare Krishna has no rule and regulation, what it means is, there is no rule and regulation for chanting Hare Krishna, but if I want rapid progress in spiritual life by chanting Hare Krishna, I should chant a minimum fixed number of times every day without fail."
**Summary:** While there are no strict rules for chanting, chanting a fixed number of rounds daily as a vow is essential for rapid spiritual progress.

But the point is, this chanting of Hare Krishna has to be done according to a certain fixed number so that we can achieve quick results, rapid results in advancing in Krishna consciousness. Although chanting Hare Krishna has no rule and regulation, what it means is, there is no rule and regulation for chanting Hare Krishna, but if I want rapid progress in spiritual life by chanting Hare Krishna, I should chant a minimum fixed number of times every day without fail. This is like how we exercise, suppose I want to develop my biceps, then I have to do certain exercises with dumbbells every day. I have to do it a fixed number of times every day, I have to count and do, one, two, three, four. When you go to the gym, you have seen people, whatever they do, they will do according to one count and they will say, yesterday I did 30 times, now today I will do 35 times, I will do 40 times, they will count and do. So if we want rapid progress in spiritual life and we want to take full advantage of the holy name of Krishna, then we have to chant a fixed number of times as a vow every day, minimum. And Prabhupada said, the basic qualification, the basic number of rounds that we all have to chant is minimum 16 malas of Hare Krishna Maha Mantra every day. Now, if you have to count every day that you are chanting 16 malas, 16 into 100. Each mala is 108 beads, 108 times you have to chant the Hare Krishna Maha Mantra. And like that you have to do 16 times. That means 1728 times you have to chant the Hare Krishna Maha Mantra every day without fail. So to help you to keep count of how many times you are chanting, it is, the Acharyas have given us the process of chanting on the beads. So chanting on the beads is a process in our Sampradaya which is given by our Acharyas by which we can count, we can keep track of the number of times I am chanting every day so that we can maintain our vow or vrata and this will ensure that we get the richest benefit of chanting Hare Krishna. Otherwise there is no rule and regulation which needs to be followed in chanting the Hare Krishna Maha Mantra. Okay?

---

## AAQ: What is the Relation Between Dvaita Advaita and Vishishtadvaita? | HG Suvyakta Narasimha Dasa
**Speaker:** HG Suvyakta Narasimha Dasa

### philosophy, Vaishnavism
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/2fg733updx8?t=0
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "What is the relationship between Dvaita, Advaita and Vishishta Advaita?"
**Summary:** The lecture begins by posing the question of the relationship between Dvaita, Advaita, and Vishishtadvaita philosophies and whether they all lead to the Supreme Lord.

What is the relationship between Dvaita, Advaita, and Vishishta Advaita? Are those three the same? Do all three lead us to the Supreme Lord?

---
### Sampradaya, Vaishnavism, Indian Philosophy
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/2fg733updx8?t=25
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "According to the Padma Purana, there are four Sampradayas which are actually bona fide, which is referring to the four systems of philosophy."
**Summary:** The speaker identifies five recognized systems of philosophy in the Indian context, focusing on the four bona fide Vaishnava Sampradayas according to the Padma Purana: Sri, Brahma, Kumara, and Rudra Sampradayas, associating them with specific Acharyas and philosophical systems.

There are practically five systems of philosophy which are recognized to be bona fide, which are recognized as valid systems of philosophy in the Indian context. One is Dvaita, one is Suddha Advaita, then there is Vishishtha Advaita, then there is Dvaita Advaita and there is Keval Advaita, which is Shankara's Sampradaya. According to the Padma Purana, there are four Sampradayas which are actually bona fide, which is referring to the four systems of philosophy: Dvaita, Vishishtha Advaita, Dvaita Advaita and Suddha Advaita. So these four systems of philosophy are propounded by four Vaishnava Acharyas. So these are the Sri Sampradaya, the Madhva Sampradaya, which is the Brahma Sampradaya, then there is the Kumara Sampradaya and there is the Rudra Sampradaya. Vishnu Swami is the main Acharya for the Rudra Sampradaya. For the Kumara Sampradaya, it is Vishnu Swami. And for Sri Sampradaya, it is Ramanuja Acharya. And for Brahma Sampradaya, it is Madhva Acharya. These are the four systems of philosophy which are declined to be bona fide in the Puranas. The fifth Sampradaya, which is founded by Shankaracharya, Adi Shankaracharya, is called Mayavada and it is not recognized as a bona fide philosophy in the Indian system. So all these four systems of philosophy, Dvaita, Vishishta Dvaita, Shuddha Dvaita, Dvaita Dvaita, these four systems of philosophy come in the Sri Sampradaya, Kumara Sampradaya, Rudra Sampradaya and Brahma Sampradaya. These four systems of philosophy, all of them are Vaishnava philosophy.

---
### Vaishnavism, Liberation, Mayavada
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/2fg733updx8?t=163
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Whereas Adi Shankara's philosophy of Kevala Dvaita is aiming at merging into Brahman. There is no individual existence."
**Summary:** The speaker explains that the four Vaishnava Sampradayas all accept Vishnu as the Supreme Personality of Godhead and aim for eternal service in Vaikuntha after liberation, contrasting this with Adi Shankara's Kevala Advaita, which aims for merging into impersonal Brahman.

They all accept Vishnu as the Supreme Personality of Godhead. And all of them say that when we achieve liberation or moksha or mukti, we go to the spiritual world, Vaikuntha, where we will continue our individuality as an eternal servant of the Supreme Lord. Now, in terms of certain finer details of the philosophy, there may be some differences between these four systems, but ultimately, all of these four are aiming towards the Supreme Personality of Godhead. Whereas Adi Shankara's philosophy of Kevala Dvaita is aiming at merging into Brahman. There is no individual existence. The Supreme Absolute Truth is some impersonal Nirakar Brahman, whereas all the other four Vaishnava systems of philosophy, they declare that the Supreme Absolute Truth is a person. It's not just some impersonal Brahman. And we are Atma. We can never become equal to Paramatma. We will always remain Paramatma as a fragmental portion of that Supreme Absolute Truth. And we will always remain as a small Jeeva, whereas Vishnu or Narayana, he is the Paramatma. He is the Supreme Personality. So this is the fundamental difference between the Mayavada philosophy and the combined all the four Vaishnava philosophies.

---
### Krishna, Madhva Sampradaya, Gaudiya Vaishnavism
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/2fg733updx8?t=264
**Verse References:** BG 10.8
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Krishna says that he is the source of everything else. Sarvam pravartate, everything is emanating from Krishna."
**Summary:** The speaker discusses the finer differences within the four Vaishnava philosophies, highlighting the Gaudiya Vaishnava philosophy's unique acceptance of Krishna as the Supreme Personality of Godhead, based on scriptural evidence like Krishna's declaration in the Bhagavad Gita that He is the source of everything.

Now within the four Vaishnava philosophies, there are finer differences, very minor, they are not major, but all of them accept Narayana as the Supreme Personality of Godhead. In the Gaudiya Vaishnava philosophy, which is Achintya Veda Veda Tattva, which is a sub-sect of the Madhva philosophy, of the Brahma Sampradaya, we accept Krishna as the Supreme Personality of Godhead. All the other four, it is Narayana. So, Krishna being the Avatari, being the Supreme Personality of Godhead, on the basis of scriptural evidence, is accepted only in the Brahma Madhva Gaudiya Vaishnava Sampradaya. All four are Vaishnava Sampradayas, but amongst the Vaishnava Sampradayas also, there are some differences. According to the other Vaishnava Sampradayas, Krishna is supposed to be an Avatara, incarnation. But in the Madhva Gaudiya Vaishnava Sampradaya, we understand Krishna is not an incarnation, because there are references. Krishna himself tells, declares in the Bhagavad Gita, am sarvasya prabhavo mattaha sarvam pravartate. Krishna says that he is the source of everything else. Sarvam pravartate, everything is emanating from Krishna. Everything means everything, including Narayana and Vishnu.

---
### Krishna, Bhagavatam, Vaishnava Sampradaya
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/2fg733updx8?t=343
**Verse References:** BG 7.7, SB 1.3.28
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Krishna says that there is nothing above him, that means even Narayana is not above him."
**Summary:** The speaker continues to establish Krishna as the Supreme Personality of Godhead according to the Madhva Gaudiya Vaishnava Sampradaya, citing Krishna's declarations in the Bhagavad Gita and the Srimad Bhagavatam's description of Krishna as the source of all incarnations, emphasizing the common goal of all four Vaishnava Sampradayas to reach Vaikuntha and serve Narayana or Krishna.

Not only that, Krishna tells in the Bhagavad Gita also, Krishna declares openly, there is nothing above me, Krishna says. So when Krishna is saying, nothing is above him, that means even Narayana is not above him. Not only that, in the Bhagavatam, Bhagavatam lists 22 incarnations. Bhagavatam lists all the Purusha Avataras also. Vishnu's incarnation, three forms of Vishnu are incarnations: Karanodaka Sai Vishnu, and then Garbhodaka Sai Vishnu, Sri Rodaka Sai Vishnu, apart from that, all the other incarnations, Lord Ramachandra, Narsimha, Vamana, Kurma, Meena, all of them are listed. At the end of listing all of them, the Bhagavatam says, eteya chamsa kalahapamsa krishnastu bhagavan svayam. That all these lists of incarnations are expansions, or expansions of the expansions of Krishna, but Krishna himself is not an incarnation. He is not an avatara, he is an avatari. He is the source of all incarnations. He is the fountainhead of all incarnations. So you see, the Bhagavatam also very clearly establishes that Krishna is the Supreme Personality of Godhead. And, so there are many other verses also in the Bhagavatam. So, like this, there may be some finer differences of opinion in the philosophies of the four systems, but ultimately, the four Vaishnava systems all accept that the Supreme Absolute Truth is a person, Paramatma, Narayana or Krishna, in Vaikuntha, Goloka, Vrindavan, and the goal of the human form of life is to go to the spiritual world, Vaikuntha, and remain as a person, as a personal servant to Narayana or Krishna. So, this everybody answer. That's the fundamental common ground, but in the finer details, there may be some differences. Okay? So, all the four Vaishnava Sampradayas, anybody who follows the bona fide parampara in any one of these four Sampradayas, Brahma Sampradaya, Shri Sampradaya, Kumara Sampradaya, Rudra Sampradaya, they will all go to Vaikuntha or to Krishna. So, this is possible in one of these four Sampradayas, but not in the Shankaracharya system of his parampara. Alright? Thank you very much. Thank you very much.

---

## AAQ: Why Did My Initial Attraction to Krishna Fade Over Time? | His Grace Suvyakta Narasimha Dasa
**Speaker:** HG Suvyakta Narasimha Dasa

### emotional attachment, devotional service

**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** story
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "But as I am doing this devotional service, I lost that emotional rasa."
**Summary:** A devotee describes losing their initial emotional connection to Krishna through devotional service and seeks understanding.

Initially, I used to be so much attached to Krishna. I used to cry most of the time because of separation from Radha Krishna. But as I am doing this devotional service, I lost that emotional rasa. How do I understand this?

---
### Bhakti Yoga, Chaitanya Mahaprabhu, example
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/RgEptXDPKPI?t=30
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Krishna wanted to teach the living entities by his personal example."
**Summary:** The speaker explains that Chaitanya Mahaprabhu, Krishna himself, appeared to teach Bhakti Yoga through his personal example.

Okay, see Chaitanya Mahaprabhu, Krishna himself, when he appeared 500 years ago, although Krishna himself, he came in the role of a devotee. He wanted to, Krishna wanted to personally set an example of how a devotee has to render devotional service to Krishna, how to execute Bhakti Yoga. Krishna wanted to teach the living entities by his personal example.

---
### teaching, analogy, example
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/RgEptXDPKPI?t=73
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** analogy
**Audience:** Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers, General public
**Key Quote:** "So that the children can learn how to write A, B, C, D."
**Summary:** The speaker uses the analogy of a teacher teaching alphabets to explain Krishna's role as Chaitanya Mahaprabhu.

Just like when we go to school, the teacher will take a chalk and write on the board, write A like this, B like this, C like this. So the teacher knows much more than A, B, C, D, but she takes the role of somebody who is learning how to write alphabets so that the children can learn how to write A, B, C, D.

---
### Bhakti Yoga, Bhagavad Gita, Chaitanya Mahaprabhu
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/RgEptXDPKPI?t=106
**Verse References:** BG 6
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "So to teach that, Krishna came down as Chaitanya Mahaprabhu."
**Summary:** Krishna established Bhakti Yoga as the topmost yoga in the Bhagavad Gita, and then descended as Chaitanya Mahaprabhu to teach how to execute it.

So in a similar way, Krishna explained in the Bhagavad Gita that rendering Bhakti Yoga is the topmost yoga system. In the sixth chapter of the Bhagavad Gita, Krishna said, one who renders Bhakti Yoga amongst all the yogis, he is the topmost. So Krishna is established in the Bhagavad Gita that Bhakti Yoga is the topmost yoga system. But now, how to execute that Bhakti Yoga? So to teach that, Krishna came down as Chaitanya Mahaprabhu, and by his personal example, he taught how to execute Bhakti Yoga.

---
### Chaitanya Mahaprabhu, Shad Goswamis, literature
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/RgEptXDPKPI?t=162
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "They wrote extensively about the philosophy of Bhakti Yoga."
**Summary:** Chaitanya Mahaprabhu focused on setting an example, while his disciples, the Shad Goswamis, especially Rupa and Sanatana Goswami, wrote extensively on Bhakti Yoga philosophy.

So, Chaitanya Mahaprabhu, although by his personal behavior and example, he set the example of how a devotee should execute his devotion service to Krishna. Chaitanya Mahaprabhu himself did not write much literature, philosophy. He left it to his disciples, the Shad Goswamis, especially Rupa Goswami and Sanatana Goswami. They wrote extensively about the philosophy of Bhakti Yoga. But Chaitanya Mahaprabhu himself, if you see, he left behind only the Six Ashtaka, eight verses which he composed himself.

---
### Chaitanya Mahaprabhu, aspiration, Bhakti Yoga
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/RgEptXDPKPI?t=207
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "This is the aspiration that every Bhakti Yogi should have."
**Summary:** The speaker describes Chaitanya Mahaprabhu's prayer for tears and ecstasy while chanting Krishna's name, highlighting it as the aspiration of every Bhakti Yoga practitioner.

In this Six Ashtaka, eight verses, one of the things that Chaitanya Mahaprabhu prays is that when will that day come when I chant Krishna's name and torrents of tears will flow down my eyes, and when I chant Krishna's name, my whole body will tremble out of great ecstasy. So, this is what Chaitanya Mahaprabhu wrote. This is the aspiration that every Bhakti Yogi should have. When I chant Krishna's holy name, when I chant Hare Krishna Mahamantra, tears should flow from my eyes. My voice should get choked up. My body, my hands, legs should all be trembling in great ecstasy.

---
### transcendental emotions, material desires, purification
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/RgEptXDPKPI?t=259
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "As long as these things rule our heart, you cannot experience those transcendental emotions of bhava bhakti."
**Summary:** The speaker explains that initial feelings of separation from Krishna are transcendental, but cannot be sustained as long as material desires like lust, greed, and fame dominate the heart.

So, naturally, the devotee who has written here, when in the beginning, when he started Krishna consciousness, when he started chanting, he used to cry out of separation from Radha Krishna, but slowly, that emotion has disappeared. What went wrong? Looks like I was better off before I started chanting Hare Krishna. Well, fact is, those feelings of separation from Radha and Krishna, by which when we chant Hare Krishna, our throat will get choked up, tears will flow from our eyes, and these kind of emotions, these kind of bhava, which come in our heart, are on the transcendental platform. They are not on the material platform. As long as in our heart, there is lust and there is greed, there is desire to enjoy in the material world, there is attraction to the opposite sex, there is a great desire to become famous, to be honored by others, to be respected by others, as long as these things are in the heart, as long as kama, krodha, lobha, mada, moha, matsarya, these things rule our heart, as long as there is a greed in our heart to become wealthy, I should get a good job, I should start a good business, I should become a good entrepreneur, I should have a unicorn from my startup, or I should get a job, I should go abroad, I should earn a lot of money, as long as these things rule our heart, you cannot experience those transcendental emotions of bhava bhakti.

---
### bhava bhakti, bodily concept of life, purification
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/RgEptXDPKPI?t=374
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** philosophy
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Until one comes out of the bodily concept of life, there is no question of experiencing real bhava bhakti."
**Summary:** Real feelings of separation from Krishna will not take root until one overcomes the six enemies (lust, anger, greed, illusion, madness, envy) and transcends the bodily concept of life.

Real feelings of separation from Krishna will not take root within the heart. Initially, some people may experience such things, but these are not permanent emotions, these are some temporary feelings that one may develop at the beginning of Krishna consciousness, out of some emotion, out of some sentiment. But real feelings, real love for Krishna comes when we have overcome all these six enemies which are ruling our heart, kama, krodha, lobha, mada, moha and matsarya, and we have come out of the bodily concept of life. Have we really come out of the bodily concept of life? That's the question we should ask ourselves. Have I realized I am not the body, I am a spirit soul, I am atma? Have I realized I am not the body, have I come out of this conception that I am this body, I am a man, I am a woman, I am an Indian, I am an American, I am a Christian, I am a Muslim, I am a Hindu? Have we come out of all these things? Until one comes out of the bodily concept of life, there is no question of experiencing real bhava bhakti, real transcendental emotions for Krishna cannot take place.

---
### detachment, vaidhi bhakti, taste
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/RgEptXDPKPI?t=465
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** practical
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Then you will develop ruchi, you will develop taste for that."
**Summary:** The speaker advises not to worry about the loss of initial emotions, as they were likely sentimental, and emphasizes the importance of practicing Krishna consciousness to develop detachment and taste for devotional service.

So, we don't have to be concerned that initially I was, you know, I used to cry in separation from Radha Krishna, but now those feelings are not there. We don't have to worry. All such feelings are on the material platform, are on a sentimental platform. You go on practicing Krishna consciousness, gradually you will come out of the bodily concept of life. First you will, in your heart, what will develop is vairagya, detachment from all the material, material desires. Then comes freedom from greed. Then comes freedom from lust. Then you will come out of the bodily concept of life. And then further you have to progress, you have to become firmly fixed up in your devotional life. You have to follow the principles of vaidhi bhakti, rules and regulations of bhakti, vaidhi bhakti. And when you become firmly fixed up in following those rules, rules and regulations of bhakti, what will happen? You will develop ruchi, you will develop taste for that.

---
### devotional service, taste, bhava bhakti
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/RgEptXDPKPI?t=533
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** practical
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers
**Key Quote:** "Real transcendental emotions, those feelings of separation from Radha and Krishna will start manifesting."
**Summary:** Developing a taste for practical devotional service leads to real transcendental emotions and feelings of separation from Radha and Krishna, which is an advanced stage requiring purification.

You will experience a certain kind of happiness in executing practical devotional service. If I have to wipe the floor of the temple hall, I have to sweep the temple hall, I have to render some practical devotional service like this, I will develop a taste for it. It will become greatly pleasurable for me. Nobody has to tell me as a rule, you have to follow the four regulated principles, you have to fast on ekadashi, you have to fast on janmashtami. You will be waiting for that opportunity. So, you have to come to that point of taste. And when further you develop that taste, then slowly it will transform into bhava bhakti. Real transcendental emotions, those feelings of separation from Radha and Krishna will start manifesting. And when that happens, whenever we chant Krishna's name, currents of tears will flow down our eyes like it's pouring rain, and we will, you know, our voice will get choked up and all those emotions will come. So, there is a time and place for that. That's a very advanced stage, and we have to first undergo purification at the lower stages to be able to come to that. So, we don't have to worry about this temporary emotions which we used to experience, but they are no more there.

---
### devotional service, purification, spiritual progress
**Timestamp:** https://youtu.be/RgEptXDPKPI?t=627
**Verse References:** —
**Content Type:** practical
**Audience:** Full-time devotees, Congregation / volunteers, Newcomers
**Key Quote:** "Continue executing your devotional service, continue chanting Hare Krishna, following the regulative principles, reading Prabhupada's books, gradually those transcendental real emotions will manifest."
**Summary:** The speaker concludes by encouraging continued devotional service, chanting, following regulative principles, and reading Prabhupada's books, emphasizing that real transcendental emotions will eventually manifest and remain permanently.

So, first we have to become practically strong and established on the platform that I am not the body, I am spirit soul. That comes first. Then other things will follow. So, simple thing, continue executing your devotional service, continue chanting Hare Krishna, following the regulative principles, reading Prabhupada's books, gradually those transcendental real emotions will manifest. And once they manifest, they will never go away. That's the truth. Subtitles by the Amara.org community.
